events.text: The patient is a 49-year-old woman named Denise Okafor. She has a family history significant for her mother with ovarian cancer at age 52 and a maternal aunt with breast cancer at age 44. She is of West African descent, non-smoker, BMI 27.4, ECOG performance status 0 at baseline. She works as a high school biology teacher.

At age 49 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IIB (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition) INVASIVE DUCTAL CARCINOMA, GRADE 3 (MALIGNANT, PRIMARY) of the LEFT BREAST, UPPER OUTER QUADRANT (ICD-O site code C50.4). Histology code: 8500/3 (Infiltrating duct carcinoma, NOS). ER: Negative (Allred score 0/8); PR: Negative (Allred score 0/8); HER2: Negative (IHC 1+, FISH not amplified, ratio 1.4); Ki-67: 85%; Nuclear grade: 3; Nottingham grade: 3 (tubule formation 3, nuclear pleomorphism 3, mitotic count 3). Tumor size on imaging: 3.2 cm. Ipsilateral axillary lymph node involvement: 1 of 3 sentinel nodes positive on initial biopsy. Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs): 35% stromal. PD-L1 CPS (22C3 assay): 18. Confirmation: Positive histology by core needle biopsy. Laterality: Left. Multifocality: Unifocal. Lymphovascular invasion: Present.

At age 49, the patient had a core needle biopsy pathology result of the left breast mass (upper outer quadrant). Gross description: Three core biopsy fragments, each approximately 1.5 cm in length. Microscopic: Infiltrating ductal carcinoma, poorly differentiated (Nottingham grade 3/3). Tumor cells show marked nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, and abundant mitoses (>20 per 10 HPF). Background shows areas of central necrosis and pushing borders. Associated high-grade ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) present at periphery (~10% of specimen). Immunohistochemistry panel: ER (SP1): negative (0% staining); PR (1E2): negative (0% staining); HER2 (4B5): 1+ incomplete membranous staining; Ki-67: 85% nuclear positivity. Stromal TILs estimated at 35%. No lymphovascular invasion identified on biopsy cores. Sentinel lymph node biopsy (same session): 1 of 3 nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma, largest deposit 4 mm, no extranodal extension.

At age 49, the patient had a diagnostic mammogram and bilateral breast MRI, which showed a 3.2 x 2.8 cm irregular, spiculated mass in the left breast upper outer quadrant with marked enhancement kinetics (type III curve, washout). Associated skin thickening overlying the mass. No satellite lesions. Left axillary lymph nodes: two enlarged nodes with cortical thickening up to 8 mm, suspicious for metastatic involvement. Right breast: no suspicious findings. BIRADS 5. No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT performed separately and showed no visceral or distant nodal metastases.

At age 49, the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for staging workup, which showed no evidence of distant metastatic disease. No pulmonary nodules, hepatic lesions, or suspicious osseous lesions. Left axillary lymphadenopathy consistent with known nodal involvement. No mediastinal or supraclavicular adenopathy. Impression: Stage IIB disease, no distant metastases identified.

At age 49 years, the patient had next generation sequencing (tumor/normal paired) performed for invasive ductal carcinoma, triple-negative subtype, based on a specimen obtained from the left breast core biopsy. Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 11 mutations/Mb (intermediate). Microsatellite status: MSS. Germline findings: BRCA2 pathogenic variant c.5946delT (p.S1982Rfs*22) confirmed in peripheral blood. Somatic alterations identified: TP53 p.R248W (VAF 42%, pathogenic, loss of function), PIK3CA p.H1047R (VAF 18%, pathogenic, gain of function), RB1 splice site c.1386+1G>A (VAF 31%, likely pathogenic, loss of function), PTEN p.R130Q (VAF 24%, likely pathogenic), MYC amplification (copy number 12, gain), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, CCNE1 amplification (copy number 6), RAD51C p.L138F (VAF 15%, variant of uncertain significance). No EGFR, KRAS, NRAS, BRAF, CDH1, or AKT1 mutations. No NTRK, ALK, or RET fusions detected. PD-L1 CPS 18 (22C3). Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) score: 68 (positive, cutoff >42). Genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH): 22%. Note: germline BRCA2 result communicated to genetic counselor for family counseling.

At age 49, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board discussion and oncology consultation. Diagnosis of Stage IIB (T2N1M0) TNBC confirmed. PD-L1 CPS 18 and germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant noted. TILs 35%, HRD positive. Plan for neoadjuvant chemotherapy with pembrolizumab per KEYNOTE-522 regimen: pembrolizumab 200 mg Q3W x 4 cycles with dose-dense AC (doxorubicin 60 mg/m2 + cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 Q2W x 4 cycles with G-CSF support), followed by pembrolizumab 200 mg Q3W + weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 x 12 weeks. Definitive surgery planned after neoadjuvant completion. Genetic counseling referral placed for BRCA2 mutation carrier management and family counseling. Patient enrolled in BRCA2 registry. Olaparib discussed as potential future therapy given BRCA2 germline status.

At age 49 years, the patient received neoadjuvant-intent pembrolizumab 200 mg IV Q3W (Cycle 1 Day 1), with concurrent dose-dense doxorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV and cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 IV (ddAC Cycle 1), plus pegfilgrastim 6 mg SC on Day 2 for G-CSF support. Pre-medications: ondansetron, dexamethasone, lorazepam. Baseline LFTs, CBC, TSH all within normal limits. Treatment tolerated with grade 1 nausea day 1-3.

At age 49, the patient developed a grade 2 immune-related adverse event: autoimmune thyroiditis (thyrotoxic phase) during pembrolizumab Cycle 2 of the neoadjuvant phase. TSH suppressed at 0.01 mIU/L (normal 0.4-4.0), free T4 elevated at 2.8 ng/dL (normal 0.8-1.8). Patient reported palpitations, heat intolerance, and fatigue. Propranolol 10 mg TID initiated for symptomatic management. Pembrolizumab held for one cycle per protocol. Endocrinology consulted. Repeat TFTs at 6 weeks showed transition to hypothyroid phase (TSH 18.2 mIU/L, free T4 0.6 ng/dL); levothyroxine 75 mcg daily initiated. Pembrolizumab resumed after normalization of thyroid function on replacement therapy.

At age 49 years, the patient completed all 4 cycles of dose-dense AC (ddAC Cycles 1-4 administered Q2W with pegfilgrastim support) concurrent with pembrolizumab (Cycles 1, 3, and 4, with Cycle 2 held for thyroiditis). Total neoadjuvant AC phase: approximately 8 weeks. Then transitioned to weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 IV x 12 weeks combined with pembrolizumab 200 mg IV Q3W (Cycles 5, 6, 7 at weeks 1, 4, 7 of paclitaxel phase). Paclitaxel dose reduced to 64 mg/m2 (80% dose) from week 7 onward due to grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy. All 12 planned weekly paclitaxel infusions completed.

At age 49, the patient developed grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy (bilateral hands and feet, stocking-glove distribution) during weekly paclitaxel, first noted at week 7 of the paclitaxel phase. Symptoms included numbness and tingling without pain or functional impairment. Paclitaxel dose reduced to 80% (64 mg/m2). Duloxetine 30 mg daily initiated for neuropathic symptoms. Patient continued all 12 planned paclitaxel doses despite dose reduction. Neuropathy improved to grade 1 by end of treatment.

At age 49, the patient had a breast MRI post-neoadjuvant chemotherapy (after completion of ddAC + paclitaxel + pembrolizumab), which showed partial radiographic response. Residual enhancing mass in left breast upper outer quadrant measuring 1.6 x 1.4 cm (previously 3.2 x 2.8 cm), representing approximately 50% reduction in size. Residual left axillary lymph node: 1 node with persistent cortical thickening, 6 mm (previously 8 mm). Overall impression: Partial radiographic response, Miller-Payne grade 3 estimated. Residual disease present; surgical planning recommended. No new distant lesions.

At age 49, the patient had a pre-operative oncology assessment. Post-neoadjuvant MRI shows partial response with residual 1.6 cm mass and persistent axillary node. Patient counseled regarding surgical options: left breast-conserving surgery (lumpectomy) with sentinel lymph node biopsy vs. mastectomy. Given BRCA2 carrier status and residual disease, patient elected left mastectomy with immediate tissue expander reconstruction and right prophylactic mastectomy with reconstruction. Breast surgery and plastic surgery teams coordinated. ECOG PS 0 at pre-op. Levothyroxine dose stabilized, TSH 1.8 mIU/L. Neuropathy grade 1, not a surgical contraindication.

At age 49 years, the patient underwent left modified radical mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection (levels I-II) and right prophylactic mastectomy with bilateral immediate tissue expander reconstruction. Procedure performed under general anesthesia without complications. Left axillary dissection yielded 16 nodes. Intraoperative findings: palpable firm residual mass in upper outer quadrant left breast. Skin-sparing mastectomy performed bilaterally with nipple-sparing on the right. Estimated blood loss 220 mL. No intraoperative complications. Drains placed bilaterally.

At age 49, the patient had a surgical mastectomy specimen pathology result. Left breast modified radical mastectomy: Residual invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, measuring 1.8 cm in greatest dimension (ypT1c). Margins: negative (closest margin 0.8 cm, deep margin). Associated residual high-grade DCIS present (2.5 cm span). Lymphovascular invasion: present (focal). Axillary lymph nodes: 3 of 16 lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma (ypN1a); largest deposit 5 mm; no extranodal extension. Residual carcinoma in sentinel node previously identified: viable tumor. Tumor bed fibrosis present (approximately 40% of tumor bed). Pathologic response classification: Non-pCR (ypT1cN1a). Miller-Payne grade 3 (marked reduction in tumor cellularity, 30-90% reduction). Residual Cancer Burden (RCB) calculated: RCB index 2.8, RCB Class III (extensive residual disease). Right prophylactic mastectomy: No carcinoma, benign fibrocystic changes only.

At age 49, the patient had a post-operative oncology assessment at 4 weeks following bilateral mastectomy. Healing well, drains removed at week 2. Tissue expanders in place bilaterally, initial fills performed. Pathology results reviewed: RCB Class III residual disease, 3/16 positive nodes (ypT1cN1a). Plan: adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 BID days 1-14 Q21 days x 8 cycles, and pembrolizumab 200 mg Q3W x 9 cycles (to complete total of 1 year of pembrolizumab including neoadjuvant doses). Post-mastectomy radiation therapy (PMRT) discussed: given 3 positive lymph nodes post-neoadjuvant therapy, radiation oncology consultation obtained. PMRT to left chest wall and regional lymph nodes recommended. Olaparib adjuvant therapy discussed; given concurrent capecitabine and ongoing pembrolizumab, plan to consider olaparib after completion of capecitabine if patient remains disease-free. Levothyroxine continued.

At age 49 years, the patient received post-mastectomy radiation therapy (PMRT) to the left chest wall and regional lymph nodes (supraclavicular, axillary levels I-III, internal mammary nodes). Technique: 3D-conformal with bolus. Total dose: 50 Gy in 25 fractions (2 Gy/fraction) to chest wall and regional nodes, followed by chest wall boost of 10 Gy in 5 fractions (total chest wall dose 60 Gy). Treatment initiated 6 weeks post-surgery, concurrent with capecitabine (capecitabine held during boost week). Radiation completed over 6.5 weeks. Acute toxicities: grade 2 radiation dermatitis (moist desquamation in axillary fold, managed with silver sulfadiazine and wound care), grade 1 fatigue.

At age 49 years, the patient received adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 BID (approximately 1500 mg BID given BSA 1.73 m2) on days 1-14 of each 21-day cycle for a planned 8 cycles, concurrent with pembrolizumab 200 mg IV Q3W (completing remaining pembrolizumab cycles to reach 17 total cycles over approximately 1 year including neoadjuvant). Capecitabine initiated 5 weeks post-surgery, 1 week before radiation. Concurrent PMRT administered (capecitabine held during radiation boost week). Cycles 1-4 completed with dose modifications as noted.

At age 49, the patient developed grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) during capecitabine Cycle 3. Findings: bilateral palmar erythema, tenderness, and superficial blistering without impairment of activities of daily living (grade 2). Capecitabine dose reduced to 75% (1125 mg BID) for subsequent cycles. Urea-based cream (Kerasal) applied twice daily; patient instructed to avoid tight footwear and prolonged pressure. Symptoms improved to grade 1 with dose reduction. No further dose reductions required.

At age 49, the patient developed a grade 3 immune-related adverse event during pembrolizumab Cycle 14 (adjuvant phase): immune-related hepatitis. ALT 412 U/L (10x ULN), AST 389 U/L (9.7x ULN), total bilirubin 1.4 mg/dL (normal). Patient asymptomatic, identified on routine LFT monitoring. Pembrolizumab permanently discontinued per protocol guidelines for grade 3 hepatitis. Methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day IV initiated for 3 days then transitioned to prednisone 1 mg/kg/day PO (80 mg daily). Hepatology consultation obtained. Hepatitis B/C serology, ANA, ASMA, anti-LKM antibodies checked; all negative. Liver biopsy deferred given classic presentation and rapid response to steroids. LFTs normalized over 6 weeks with steroid taper. Prednisone tapered over 8 weeks to discontinuation. Capecitabine continued uninterrupted during hepatitis management.

At age 49, the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast at 6-month surveillance (approximately 6 months post-surgery), which showed no evidence of local recurrence or distant metastatic disease. Left chest wall post-mastectomy changes consistent with tissue expander in place. Post-radiation changes in left chest wall soft tissues. No hepatic lesions (liver enzymes normalizing on steroids). No pulmonary nodules or mediastinal adenopathy. No suspicious osseous lesions. Impression: No evidence of disease.

At age 49, the patient had an oncology follow-up visit after completing capecitabine (8 cycles completed, total duration ~24 weeks) and pembrolizumab permanently discontinued after grade 3 hepatitis at cycle 14. ECOG PS 0. Neuropathy: grade 1, improved. Hand-foot syndrome: resolved. Thyroid function stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg. LFTs now normalized (ALT 28 U/L, AST 24 U/L). Patient on steroid taper (prednisone 20 mg, tapering). Discussing olaparib adjuvant therapy given germline BRCA2 status and RCB Class III residual disease at surgery. OlympiA trial data reviewed with patient: 3-year IDFS benefit with olaparib 300 mg BID x 1 year in germline BRCA1/2 TNBC with high-risk features. Plan: start olaparib after prednisone taper complete and LFTs remain normal. Tissue expander exchange to permanent implants scheduled with plastic surgery.

At age 50 years, the patient initiated adjuvant olaparib 300 mg BID (OlympiA regimen) following completion of all other adjuvant therapies and normalization of liver enzymes. Indication: germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant, high-risk residual disease (RCB Class III, node-positive post-neoadjuvant). CBC and LFTs normal at baseline. Planned duration: 1 year (approximately 52 weeks). Patient counseled on anemia risk, nausea, fatigue. Started with CBC monitoring every 4 weeks for first 3 months.

At age 50, the patient developed grade 2 anemia during olaparib therapy at week 8. Hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL (baseline 12.4 g/dL), MCV 102 fL (macrocytic pattern). Reticulocyte count low-normal. B12 and folate normal. Iron studies: ferritin 45 ng/mL, TIBC elevated, consistent with functional iron deficiency. Olaparib continued at full dose; IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose 750 mg x1) administered. Hemoglobin improved to 10.2 g/dL at 4-week recheck. No transfusion required. Olaparib dose not reduced. Repeat CBC at 12 weeks showed Hgb 11.1 g/dL, stable.

At age 50, the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast at 12-month surveillance post-surgery (during olaparib therapy), which showed no evidence of local recurrence or distant metastatic disease. No new pulmonary nodules. Liver appears normal in size and morphology, no hepatic lesions. Bilateral breast implants in place post exchange surgery. No suspicious lymphadenopathy. Post-radiation soft tissue changes in left chest wall stable. Left axillary surgical changes stable. Impression: No evidence of disease at 12 months post-surgery.

At age 50 years, the patient underwent bilateral tissue expander exchange to permanent silicone implants (Mentor MemoryGel) as part of staged breast reconstruction, performed by plastic surgery. Left breast reconstruction following therapeutic mastectomy; right reconstruction following prophylactic mastectomy. Procedure uneventful, performed as outpatient. No complications. Patient highly satisfied with cosmetic outcome. Neuropathy grade 1 at time of surgery, not a contraindication.

At age 50, the patient had a routine oncology follow-up at completion of 1 year of adjuvant olaparib. All 52 weeks of olaparib completed at full dose (300 mg BID) without further dose reductions. Final CBC on olaparib: Hgb 11.8 g/dL, platelets 198 x10^9/L, WBC 5.1 x10^9/L. LFTs normal. ECOG PS 0. No evidence of disease on imaging at 12 months post-surgery. Peripheral neuropathy: grade 1, stable, duloxetine 30 mg daily ongoing. Thyroid function stable on levothyroxine. Reconstruction complete, patient satisfied. Bilateral implants intact on exam. Premenopausal status: last menstrual period occurred approximately 4 months after chemotherapy completion (transient chemotherapy-induced amenorrhea), cycles now irregular; referral to gynecologic oncology for discussion of risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy given BRCA2 status. Plan: surveillance imaging Q6 months for 2 years, then annually. Continue levothyroxine and duloxetine.

At age 50, the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast at 18-month surveillance post-surgery, which showed no evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic disease. New finding: small 4 mm pulmonary nodule in right lower lobe, not present on prior scans. Given small size and no other suspicious findings, Fleischner Society guidelines recommend follow-up CT in 3 months. No hepatic lesions, no adenopathy, no osseous lesions. Impression: No evidence of malignant disease; 4 mm RLL pulmonary nodule, likely benign, short-interval follow-up recommended.

At age 50, the patient had a follow-up CT chest (3-month interval follow-up for 4 mm RLL nodule), which showed stable 4 mm right lower lobe pulmonary nodule, no interval growth. No new pulmonary nodules. No mediastinal or hilar adenopathy. Impression: Stable pulmonary nodule, most consistent with benign etiology (granuloma or intrapulmonary lymph node). Next surveillance per standard schedule.

At age 50, the patient had an oncology surveillance visit at 24 months post-surgery. No new symptoms. No evidence of disease on most recent imaging including stable pulmonary nodule confirmed benign. ECOG PS 0. Completing risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRBSO) planned in coordination with gynecologic oncology given BRCA2 germline variant (surgery scheduled for next month). Duloxetine continued for residual grade 1 neuropathy. Levothyroxine dose adjusted: TSH 5.1 mIU/L, dose increased to 88 mcg daily. Patient overall doing well, back to full-time work. No signs of local or distant recurrence. Next CT surveillance at 30 months. Continued multidisciplinary follow-up with genetics, gynecologic oncology, and breast surgery teams.

documents.text:
BREAST MRI WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST — POST-NEOADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY RESTAGING

CLINICAL INDICATION: 49-year-old woman with known left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, triple-negative subtype (ER/PR/HER2-negative), initially presenting as a 3.2 cm upper outer quadrant mass with ipsilateral axillary nodal involvement (T2N1M0, Stage IIB). Patient has completed neoadjuvant systemic therapy per KEYNOTE-522 protocol: dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide x 4 cycles followed by weekly paclitaxel x 12 weeks, both with concurrent pembrolizumab. History of immune-related thyroiditis during treatment; currently on levothyroxine replacement. This examination is performed to evaluate treatment response and inform surgical planning.

COMPARISON: Diagnostic bilateral breast MRI performed prior to initiation of neoadjuvant therapy.

TECHNIQUE: Bilateral breast MRI performed on a 3.0 Tesla magnet using a dedicated 16-channel bilateral breast coil. Sequences acquired include sagittal T2-weighted fat-saturated, axial T1-weighted non-fat-saturated, axial dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) T1 fat-saturated series (pre-contrast, and four sequential post-contrast acquisitions at approximately 90-second intervals), and subtraction images. Gadolinium-based contrast agent (gadobutrol 0.1 mmol/kg) administered intravenously via right antecubital vein. MIP reconstructions generated. Kinetic curve analysis performed using dedicated CAD workstation.

---

FINDINGS:

LEFT BREAST:

Known primary tumor site (upper outer quadrant, 10 o'clock position, approximately 4 cm from the nipple): There is a residual area of irregular enhancement in the left breast upper outer quadrant, measuring approximately 1.6 x 1.4 cm in its greatest cross-sectional dimensions (previously 3.2 x 2.8 cm on the pre-treatment MRI). The lesion demonstrates persistent but substantially diminished mass enhancement with spiculated margins. Kinetic curve analysis of the dominant enhancing focus reveals a type II curve (plateau pattern), compared to the prior type III (washout) curve, indicating an attenuation of the aggressive enhancement kinetics seen at baseline. The overall signal intensity of the residual mass on T2-weighted sequences is heterogeneous, with areas of low T2 signal consistent with fibrotic change interspersed with foci of intermediate signal suggesting viable residual tumor. There is no washout kinetic pattern in the dominant component. Enhancement is most pronounced in the periphery of the lesion, with the central portion showing reduced signal on subtraction series, consistent with treatment-induced fibrosis and necrosis.

There is no evidence of new satellite lesions within the left breast. No additional suspicious foci of non-mass enhancement are identified. The residual mass does not contact the overlying skin; the skin thickening previously noted overlying the mass is substantially improved and the dermal thickening measures less than 3 mm (previously up to 5 mm), consistent with treatment response. The nipple-areolar complex of the left breast appears unremarkable. No pectoralis muscle involvement identified. The distance of the residual enhancing focus from the deep (posterior) margin is approximately 0.9 cm from the pectoral fascia. No new or enlarged internal mammary lymph nodes are identified.

A small, faint area of non-mass enhancement measuring approximately 0.8 cm is noted at the posterolateral margin of the primary tumor bed and is felt to represent perilesional inflammatory/reactive change rather than a discrete satellite lesion. This was not identified on the prior study and is likely treatment-related.

RIGHT BREAST:
No suspicious mass lesion, asymmetric enhancement, or pathologic non-mass enhancement is identified. Background parenchymal enhancement is minimal to mild and symmetric. No axillary lymph nodes of concern on the right.

BILATERAL AXILLAE:
Left axilla: One lymph node is identified with persistent cortical thickening measuring approximately 6 mm (previously the largest node showed cortical thickening up to 8 mm; two such nodes were noted on the prior examination). The currently visualized node retains an eccentric, somewhat irregular cortex without a clearly identifiable fatty hilum, and its morphology remains suspicious for residual nodal involvement, though less pronounced than at baseline. A second previously suspicious left axillary node is no longer appreciably enlarged and now demonstrates a normal reniform morphology with preserved fatty hilum — this finding is consistent with interval treatment response. No new enlarged or morphologically abnormal left axillary lymph nodes identified.

Right axilla: No pathologically enlarged or morphologically suspicious lymph nodes.

Supraclavicular and infraclavicular regions: Bilateral: no suspicious adenopathy identified on limited evaluation.

MUSCULOSKELETAL AND CHEST WALL:
No suspicious signal abnormality in the visualized pectoral musculature or chest wall soft tissues bilaterally. The visualized ribs and sternum demonstrate no marrow signal abnormality to suggest osseous involvement.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Substantial but incomplete response of the left breast primary tumor to neoadjuvant chemotherapy and immunotherapy. The dominant enhancing mass in the upper outer quadrant has considerably diminished in size from 3.2 x 2.8 cm to approximately 1.6 x 1.4 cm, representing a marked reduction in the bulk of enhancing disease. The enhancement kinetics have shifted from a frankly malignant washout pattern to a plateau pattern, and there is substantial T2 signal heterogeneity consistent with treatment-related fibrosis and necrosis. Residual viable tumor cannot be excluded in the enhancing peripheral components, and no complete radiographic response is achieved.

2. Left axillary adenopathy: Interval improvement with one of two previously suspicious lymph nodes now appearing morphologically normal. One residual node with cortical thickening persists, suspicious for residual nodal metastatic disease, though less bulky than at baseline.

3. Previously noted left breast skin thickening has substantially resolved, consistent with treatment response.

4. Right breast: no suspicious findings. No contralateral axillary adenopathy.

5. No new suspicious findings within the field of view to suggest distant disease.

Overall, the imaging findings are in keeping with a meaningful but partial radiographic response to neoadjuvant therapy. Estimated tumor cellularity reduction is consistent with moderate treatment effect (Miller-Payne grade 3 range estimated radiographically). Residual enhancing disease is present in the left breast and left axilla. Surgical resection is appropriate for definitive pathologic staging and therapeutic intent.

MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CLINIC
PRE-OPERATIVE ASSESSMENT NOTE

PATIENT: Denise Okafor
ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, pre-surgical evaluation

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT
Ms. Okafor is a 49-year-old West African-descent woman with Stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant carrier, presenting today for pre-operative oncology assessment following completion of neoadjuvant chemotherapy and immunotherapy. She has completed all planned neoadjuvant treatment (ddAC x4 cycles + Keytruda, then weekly Taxol x12 weeks + pembro) and is now being evaluated prior to definitive surgery. Post-neoadjuvant MRI has been reviewed. Surgical options have been discussed with the breast surgery and plastic surgery teams and today's visit focuses on finalizing the oncologic plan, addressing patient questions, and ensuring fitness for surgery.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okafor was diagnosed with left breast TNBC approximately 5 months ago following presentation with a self-palpated left upper outer quadrant mass. Biopsy confirmed grade 3 infiltrating ductal carcinoma, ER 0%, PR 0%, HER2 1+ (non-amplified by FISH), Ki-67 85%, with 1 of 3 sentinel lymph nodes involved on initial biopsy. NGS demonstrated a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT), somatic TP53 p.R248W, PIK3CA p.H1047R, and HRD-positive status (HRD score 68). PD-L1 CPS 18 by 22C3 assay. TILs 35% stromal.

She was enrolled on the KEYNOTE-522 neoadjuvant regimen: pembro 200 mg IV Q3W x4 cycles with concurrent dose-dense doxo/cyclo Q2W (ddAC), followed by weekly Taxol 80 mg/m2 x12 weeks combined with pembro Q3W. Notable treatment course events:
- Keytruda Cycle 2 held for grade 2 immune-related thyroiditis (thyrotoxic phase transitioning to hypothyroid phase); patient now stably on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily.
- PTX dose reduced to 64 mg/m2 (80%) from week 7 onward for grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy; duloxetine 30 mg daily started for symptom management.
- All 4 ddAC cycles and all 12 planned weekly Taxol doses were completed.

Post-neoadjuvant breast MRI was performed and reviewed in detail at today's visit. Imaging shows a residual enhancing mass in the left breast upper outer quadrant measuring approximately 1.6 x 1.4 cm (down from 3.2 x 2.8 cm at baseline), representing a meaningful reduction but with clearly persistent residual disease. Residual left axillary node with cortical thickening persists at 6 mm. Radiographic impression is consistent with a partial response; no pathologic complete response is expected. No new or distant lesions identified.

Given the imaging findings -- residual mass with persistent nodal involvement -- and in the context of her germline BRCA2 status, surgical options were extensively discussed across multiple visits with the breast surgical oncology and plastic surgery teams. Ms. Okafor had a detailed conversation about breast-conserving surgery (lumpectomy) with re-excision as needed, axillary lymph node dissection (ALND), versus left modified radical mastectomy. She also expressed strong interest in contralateral prophylactic mastectomy given her BRCA2 carrier status. After considering reconstruction options, she has elected to proceed with left modified radical mastectomy (levels I-II ALND) with immediate tissue expander placement, and right prophylactic mastectomy with immediate tissue expander reconstruction. This decision was made thoughtfully over multiple discussions and is consistent with her goals and risk-reduction priorities.

Today she reports feeling well overall. She is fatigued but functional. Neuropathy in hands and feet is improved since Taxol completion -- currently grade 1 at most, with mild persistent tingling bilaterally in fingertips and toes that does not limit her activities. She is working part-time (she has taken a temporary reduced load from her teaching position). No new symptoms. No dyspnea, chest pain, or bone pain. No abdominal symptoms. Bowel and bladder function normal.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Mild residual fatigue, improving over the past 2-3 weeks since PTX completion. No fever, chills, or night sweats.
Neurologic: Persistent mild (grade 1) bilateral distal sensory neuropathy, improving. No motor deficits.
Endocrine: No palpitations, heat/cold intolerance, or tremor since initiation of levothyroxine. Last TFTs reviewed today (see below).
Cardiovascular: No dyspnea on exertion, no lower extremity edema, no palpitations.
Pulmonary: No cough, no dyspnea.
Gastrointestinal: No nausea/vomiting at this point. No diarrhea, constipation, or mucositis. Appetite improving.
Musculoskeletal: Arthralgia mild and improved since PTX completed. No focal bone pain.
Dermatologic: No rash. No palmar/plantar changes.
Psychiatric: Anxiety present but manageable; patient connected with social work. Coping well overall.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

---

MEDICATIONS (current)
- Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (immune-related hypothyroidism on Keytruda)
- Duloxetine 30 mg PO daily (chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy)
- Ondansetron 4 mg PO Q8H PRN nausea (rarely using now)
- Multivitamin daily

ALLERGIES: NKDA

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 68 bpm (regular), RR 16, Temp 36.8°C, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 75.2 kg, BSA 1.73 m2.
ECOG performance status: 0.

General: Pleasant, well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational, appropriately anxious about upcoming surgery.

Head/Neck: Anicteric sclerae. No thyromegaly on palpation. No supraclavicular or cervical lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Breast exam:
- Left breast: Firm, irregular mass palpable in upper outer quadrant, approximately 2 cm on palpation, consistent with residual tumor (imaging 1.6 cm; exam somewhat limited by density). No skin dimpling, no skin ulceration. No nipple discharge. Overlying skin intact.
- Left axilla: Single firm, slightly enlarged lymph node palpable in left axilla, approximately 1 cm. Non-tender.
- Right breast: No dominant mass, no axillary adenopathy. No skin changes.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze or crackles.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses.

Extremities: No edema. No calf tenderness.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout. Sensation: slightly diminished light touch at distal toes and fingertips bilaterally, symmetric, non-progressive. Gait normal.

Skin: No rash. No palmar erythema. No jaundice.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (reviewed today)

Specimen A — Left Breast Core Biopsy:
Sections show breast parenchyma extensively replaced by a poorly differentiated invasive carcinoma arranged in solid nests, trabeculae, and individually dispersed cells within a desmoplastic stroma. The tumor cells are large with markedly irregular nuclei, coarse chromatin, and conspicuous macronucleoli. The cytoplasm is moderate and eosinophilic. Mitotic figures are numerous and easily identified, with atypical forms present; mitotic count exceeds 20 per 10 HPF (40x objective). Areas of central geographic necrosis are noted within both the invasive component and the associated in situ component. The background stroma demonstrates a moderate lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate; stromal TILs are estimated at approximately 35% by standardized scoring.

At the periphery of the biopsy cores, foci of high-grade DCIS are identified with solid and cribriform architecture and central comedo-type necrosis. The DCIS occupies approximately 10% of the biopsy material. The ductal basement membrane appears largely intact on routine sections.

No unequivocal lymphovascular invasion (angiolymphatic emboli) is identified within the submitted biopsy cores. No perineural invasion is seen.

Specimen B — Left Axillary Core Biopsy:
Sections show residual lymph node tissue with largely effaced architecture. There are clusters and sheets of malignant epithelial cells with cytomorphology identical to the invasive carcinoma in Specimen A. The largest contiguous cluster measures approximately 4 mm. No definitive extranodal extension is identified on the submitted sections. The background lymph node tissue shows reactive sinus histiocytosis.

PATHOLOGIST'S NOTE:
The morphologic and immunophenotypic findings are diagnostic of poorly differentiated (grade 3) invasive ductal carcinoma (infiltrating duct carcinoma, NOS) of the left breast, triple-negative subtype. The high Ki-67 proliferative index, markedly elevated mitotic rate, associated high-grade DCIS with comedo necrosis, and CK5/6/EGFR positivity are all consistent with a basal-like molecular subtype. CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Ms. Okafor is a 49-year-old West African woman with Stage IIB (T2N1M0) triple-negative breast cancer who presents for oncology follow-up at the conclusion of adjuvant capecitabine and pembrolizumab. She is here today primarily to review her completed treatment course, discuss residual toxicities, and plan next steps including potential adjuvant olaparib.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okafor was originally diagnosed with a 3.2 cm left breast upper outer quadrant invasive ductal carcinoma, Nottingham grade 3, ER/PR/HER2-negative, Ki-67 85%, with one sentinel lymph node positive on initial biopsy. NGS confirmed a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT) along with somatic TP53 p.R248W, PIK3CA p.H1047R, RB1 splice-site mutation, PTEN p.R130Q, MYC amplification, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion. HRD score was 68, gLOH 22%, PD-L1 CPS 18. She received neoadjuvant pembrolizumab 200 mg Q3W plus dose-dense AC x4 cycles (ddAC Q2W with pegfilgrastim) followed by weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 x12 weeks with concurrent pembrolizumab per the KEYNOTE-522 backbone. Her neoadjuvant course was notable for two immune-related events: grade 2 autoimmune thyroiditis (thyrotoxic phase transitioning to hypothyroidism, now on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily) and grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy requiring paclitaxel dose reduction to 80% from week 7 onward, managed with duloxetine 30 mg daily.

Post-neoadjuvant breast MRI demonstrated partial radiographic response with a residual 1.6 cm enhancing mass and persistent left axillary cortical thickening. She subsequently underwent left modified radical mastectomy with ALND (levels I-II) and right prophylactic mastectomy with bilateral immediate tissue expander reconstruction. Surgical pathology confirmed non-pCR: residual IDC 1.8 cm (ypT1c), 3 of 16 left axillary nodes positive (ypN1a), LVI present, and RCB Index 2.8 (RCB Class III — extensive residual disease). The right prophylactic mastectomy showed only benign fibrocystic changes.

Given RCB Class III residual disease and three persistently positive nodes after neoadjuvant therapy, she received post-mastectomy radiation therapy to the left chest wall and regional lymph nodes (total dose 60 Gy to chest wall including boost, 50 Gy to regional nodes), completed over approximately 6.5 weeks, concurrent with adjuvant capecitabine. PMRT was complicated by grade 2 radiation dermatitis with moist desquamation in the axillary fold, which has since resolved.

Adjuvant capecitabine was started at 1500 mg BID (1000 mg/m2 BID based on BSA 1.73 m2) on a 14-days-on/7-days-off schedule Q21 days, concurrent with continuing pembrolizumab to complete her planned approximately 1 year of immunotherapy (17 total cycles inclusive of neoadjuvant). She developed grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome) during capecitabine Cycle 3, requiring a dose reduction to 75% (1125 mg BID), which brought the HFS down to grade 1. All 8 planned cycles of capecitabine were completed.

During adjuvant pembrolizumab Cycle 14, she was found to have asymptomatic grade 3 immune-related hepatitis on routine LFT monitoring (ALT 412 U/L, AST 389 U/L, bilirubin normal). Pembrolizumab was permanently discontinued at that point per standard management guidelines. She was treated with methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg IV x3 days followed by prednisone 80 mg daily PO (1 mg/kg) with a slow taper over 8 weeks. Hepatology was consulted; workup for viral hepatitis and autoimmune hepatitis serologies was negative. Capecitabine was continued uninterrupted through the hepatitis episode. LFTs have since normalized. She is currently on prednisone 20 mg daily with ongoing taper.

She completed her final (8th) cycle of capecitabine approximately 3 weeks ago. She presents today for reassessment and discussion of olaparib adjuvant therapy.

INTERPRETATION
========================================================
This tumor demonstrates a genomic profile characteristic of high-grade, triple-negative (ER-/PR-/HER2-) invasive ductal carcinoma with features of genomic instability and homologous recombination deficiency (HRD). The findings are interpreted in the context of the patient's clinical diagnosis of Stage IIB TNBC.

Key Interpretive Points:

1. GERMLINE BRCA2 PATHOGENIC VARIANT: A frameshift deletion in BRCA2 (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982ArgfsTer22) was confirmed in peripheral blood (germline origin). This is a well-characterized loss-of-function variant that abolishes BRCA2 function in homologous recombination repair (HRR). In the tumor, somatic loss of the wild-type BRCA2 allele is inferred based on the elevated HRD composite score (68) and gLOH (22%), consistent with biallelic functional inactivation. This germline finding has significant implications for the patient and first-degree relatives (50% transmission risk) and warrants formal genetic counseling. In addition to breast cancer risk (lifetime risk ~71% for BRCA2 carriers), this variant confers substantially elevated ovarian cancer risk (lifetime risk ~17-20%), as well as increased risks for pancreatic, prostate (in male relatives), and other cancers.

2. HRD-POSITIVE GENOMIC PROFILE: The HRD composite score of 68 (positive; cutoff >42) and gLOH of 22% indicate extensive genomic scarring consistent with a defect in homologous recombination repair. This is mechanistically explained by the germline BRCA2 variant combined with the somatic RAD51C missense VUS (significance uncertain). HRD positivity has therapeutic implications, particularly for sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy and PARP inhibitors.

3. TP53 p.R248W (VAF 42%): This is among the most common and well-characterized TP53 hotspot mutations, occurring at the DNA contact arginine-248 codon. The R248W substitution not only abolishes wild-type p53 tumor suppressor function but also confers a dominant-negative gain-of-function effect, interfering with residual wild-type p53 activity and promoting tumor aggressiveness, chromosomal instability, and resistance to apoptosis. High VAF (42%) suggests clonal, early event. Consistent with grade 3 histology and 85% Ki-67.

4. PIK3CA p.H1047R (VAF 18%): This is a canonical activating hotspot in the kinase domain of PIK3CA (PI3K catalytic subunit alpha), resulting in constitutive PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling. Present at sub-clonal VAF (18%), suggesting possible emergence in a subpopulation or selection under prior microenvironmental pressures. In TNBC, PIK3CA mutations may co-occur with PTEN loss (see below), compounding pathway activation.

5. RB1 Splice Site Mutation + CDKN2A Homozygous Deletion: Co-occurring RB1 loss-of-function (splice donor disruption) and homozygous CDKN2A deletion represent dual deregulation of the CDK4/6-RB cell cycle axis. Loss of both p16(INK4a) and p14(ARF) encoded by CDKN2A eliminates negative regulation of CDK4/6 (via p16) and MDM2 (via p14ARF), the latter further impairing residual p53 pathway function. Together, these alterations predict an aggressive proliferative phenotype consistent with grade 3 histology and extremely elevated Ki-67 (85%).

6. PTEN p.R130Q (VAF 24%): Missense mutation in the phosphatase domain of PTEN reduces or eliminates lipid phosphatase activity, impairing conversion of PIP3 to PIP2 and leading to unopposed PI3K/AKT pathway activation. Co-occurrence with PIK3CA p.H1047R suggests strong selection for PI3K pathway hyper-activation in this tumor.

7. MYC Amplification (copy number ~12): High-level MYC amplification at 8q24.21 drives broad transcriptional programs supporting cell growth, proliferation, metabolism, and immune evasion. Frequently observed in high-grade TNBC. Associated with aggressive biology and poor prognosis in the neoadjuvant setting.

8. CCNE1 Amplification (copy number ~6): Cyclin E1 amplification promotes CDK2-mediated S-phase entry and has been associated with replication stress, genomic instability, and relative resistance to CDK4/6 inhibitors (which are not standard of care in TNBC). May have implications for emerging CDK2-targeted therapies.

9. RAD51C p.L138F (VAF 15%, VUS): Missense variant in RAD51C (a BRCA2-interacting protein critical to RAD51 filament formation). Classified as VUS due to insufficient functional and population data. Given the already-established HRD phenotype driven by germline BRCA2 loss, the clinical significance of this somatic VUS is less determinative. No germline RAD51C pathogenic variant identified.

10. TMB 11 mut/Mb (Intermediate): Intermediate TMB. Not classified as TMB-high (generally defined as >=10 mut/Mb in some guidelines, but clinical significance of intermediate TMB in breast cancer remains investigational). PD-L1 CPS 18 (positive) is the more clinically relevant immunotherapy biomarker in this context.

11. MSS: Absence of microsatellite instability is consistent with mismatch repair-proficient tumor. MMR gene alterations not identified.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okafor is a 49-year-old woman, otherwise healthy, who presented to her primary care physician approximately 3 weeks ago after self-detecting a firm, non-tender mass in her left breast upper outer quadrant. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okafor is a 49-year-old woman with newly diagnosed Stage IIB (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th ed.) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma, left breast, with the following high-risk features: grade 3, Ki-67 85%, lymphovascular invasion on biopsy, positive sentinel node, TILs 35%, PD-L1 CPS 18 (positive), germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant, and HRD-positive tumor biology (HRD score 68, gLOH 22%). Her staging workup does not demonstrate distant metastatic disease.

This case was reviewed at multidisciplinary tumor board; the following management plan was formulated and discussed at length with the patient today.

1. NEOADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY — KEYNOTE-522 REGIMEN:
   Ms. Okafor is an excellent candidate for neoadjuvant pembro-based therapy per the KEYNOTE-522 schema. Rationale includes: PD-L1 CPS 18, TILs 35%, high-grade/high-proliferation TNBC, and resectable Stage IIB disease where pCR is the goal. The regimen will consist of:
   - Keytruda (Keytruda) 200 mg IV Q3W x 4 cycles concurrent with dose-dense doxo (Adriamycin) 60 mg/m2 + cyclo 600 mg/m2 Q2W x 4 cycles (ddAC). G-CSF support with pegfilgrastim (Neulasta) 6 mg SC on Day 2 of each ddAC cycle.
   - Followed by Keytruda 200 mg IV Q3W concurrent with weekly PTX (Taxol) 80 mg/m2 IV x 12 weeks.
   Premedications per protocol (ondansetron, dexamethasone, lorazepam). LVEF assessment (echocardiogram) to be completed prior to first anthracycline dose.
   I explained the rationale for combining immunotherapy with chemotherapy in this setting, citing the KEYNOTE-522 data demonstrating improved pathologic complete response rates and event-free survival benefit.

2. GERMLINE BRCA2 FINDINGS AND GENETIC COUNSELING:
   Pathogenic BRCA2 variant (c.5946delT) identified on tumor/normal paired NGS, confirmed in peripheral blood. I have placed a referral to genetic counseling for formal counseling, cascade testing discussion for first-degree relatives, and enrollment in the BRCA2 carrier registry. I discussed with Ms. Okafor that this result has implications beyond her current breast cancer diagnosis — including lifetime risk of ovarian cancer, contralateral breast cancer, and implications for her biological children. She expressed understanding and plans to inform her family members.
   - Lynparza (Lynparza): Given germline BRCA2 status and high-risk disease features, adjuvant ola per the OlympiA protocol is a potential future option following completion of neoadjuvant and surgical therapy. This was introduced conceptually today; definitive decision will be made based on surgical pathology response.

3. SURGICAL PLANNING:
   Definitive surgery will be planned after completion of neoadjuvant therapy. Options to be discussed with breast surgery team include left breast-conserving surgery vs. mastectomy. Given germline BRCA2 status, prophylactic surgery on the contralateral breast will also be an important discussion. She will be seen jointly with breast surgery.

4. POST-NEOADJUVANT STRATEGY:
   We discussed that the pathologic response at surgery will guide adjuvant therapy decisions. If residual disease is present, options include cape (Xeloda) per CREATE-X protocol, with continued Keytruda to complete one year of total immunotherapy. If pCR is achieved, Keytruda will be continued to complete one year per protocol.

5. MONITORING AND TOXICITY COUNSELING:
   Patient counseled on the following potential toxicities:
   - Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) with pembro: thyroiditis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, colitis, adrenal insufficiency — patient instructed on symptoms to report immediately. She was given written materials.
   - Anthracycline-related cardiotoxicity: LVEF monitoring planned.
   - Taxane-associated peripheral neuropathy: will monitor for dose modification.
   - Alopecia: anticipated with anthracycline and taxane; wigs/cold cap discussed.
   - Fertility: Ms. Okafor's family is complete; chemotherapy-induced amenorrhea discussed.
   - Bone marrow suppression: CBC monitoring schedule reviewed; pegfilgrastim for neutropenia prophylaxis.

6. FERTILITY/MENSTRUAL CONSIDERATIONS:
   Patient reports her family is complete. Chemotherapy-related ovarian suppression discussed. Given germline BRCA2 status, risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRBSO) will be considered after oncologic treatment is complete; referral to gynecologic oncology to be placed at appropriate time.

7. SUPPORTIVE CARE:
   Nutrition consultation placed. Social work referral offered and accepted (patient expressed interest in support group resources). Integrative oncology consultation available if desired.

8. RETURN VISIT:
   Patient will return in approximately one week for pre-treatment baseline labs (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH) and echocardiogram results review, and to initiate Cycle 1 of Keytruda + ddAC. Signed consent obtained today for all planned systemic agents.

Total time with patient: approximately 75 minutes. Questions answered thoroughly. Patient verbalized understanding and is ready to proceed.
